seminal_paper_arxiv: Folklore (number theory of arithmetic functions). Mathlib4 references: `IsPrimePow.deficient` in NumberTheory/FactorisationProperties.lean; `Nat.card_divisors`, `Nat.divisors_prime_pow`, `Nat.factorization_prod_pow_eq_self` in Data/Nat/Factorization.
bridge_lemma: Step 1: 1 < n + Nat.Prime (Nat.divisors n).card ⇒ IsPrimePow n, since Nat.card_divisors gives σ₀(n) = ∏_{p ∈ primeFactors n} (factorization p + 1), each factor ≥ 2, and a product of integers ≥ 2 equal to a prime forces a singleton finset. Step 2: IsPrimePow n ⇒ Deficient n by Mathlib's IsPrimePow.deficient. Step 3: Deficient n ⇒ σ(n) < 2n via Nat.sum_divisors_eq_sum_properDivisors_add_self, contradicting σ(n) = k·n for any k ≥ 2.
informal_proof_outline: Theorem: ∀ n > 1, Nat.Prime (Nat.divisors n).card → ¬ ∃ k ≥ 2, (Nat.divisors n).sum id = k * n. Step 1 (prime-card forces prime-power). By Nat.card_divisors (hn.ne_bot), (Nat.divisors n).card = ∏_{p ∈ Nat.primeFactors n} (Nat.factorization n p + 1). Each factor (factorization n p + 1) is ≥ 2 because p ∈ primeFactors implies factorization n p ≥ 1. A product of natural numbers each ≥ 2 that equals a prime q must have exactly one factor: if there were two distinct primes p₁ ≠ p₂ in primeFactors n, the product would split as (factorization n p₁ + 1) · (∏_{p ≠ p₁} (factorization n p + 1)) with both factors ≥ 2, contradicting q.isUnit_or_isUnit. Hence primeFactors n is a singleton {p}, and by Nat.factorization_prod_pow_eq_self (hn.ne_bot), n = p^(factorization n p) = p^(q-1) where q = σ₀(n). This proves IsPrimePow n. Step 2 (prime powers are deficient). Apply Mathlib's IsPrimePow.deficient: Deficient n, i.e. ∑_{d ∈ properDivisors n} d < n. Step 3 (deficiency contradicts multiperfectness). Suppose ∃ k ≥ 2 with (Nat.divisors n).sum id = k * n. By Nat.sum_divisors_eq_sum_properDivisors_add_self, the sum of divisors equals the sum of proper divisors plus n; so ∑_{properDivisors} + n = k·n, hence ∑_{properDivisors} = (k-1)·n ≥ n (since k ≥ 2), contradicting deficient < n. Hence no such k exists, and the theorem follows. The oddness hypothesis used in prior slots is irrelevant: the deficiency bound holds for all prime powers including p = 2.
candidate_lemmas: Helper one: structural collapse step. Premises 1 < n and Nat.Prime (Nat.divisors n).card jointly imply IsPrimePow n. Proof template (see slot 1297): Nat.card_divisors hn.ne_bot rewrites the cardinality as a product over Nat.primeFactors n of (Nat.factorization n p + 1); each factor is ≥ 2; a product of integers ≥ 2 equal to a prime forces the indexing finset to have card 1; Nat.factorization_prod_pow_eq_self then yields n = p^(q-1).

Helper two: deficiency pivot. Apply the existing Mathlib lemma IsPrimePow.deficient (Mathlib.NumberTheory.FactorisationProperties) directly to the conclusion of helper one. This gives Nat.Deficient n, i.e. ∑_{d ∈ Nat.properDivisors n} d < n.

Helper three: deficient excludes k ≥ 2. Use Nat.sum_divisors_eq_sum_properDivisors_add_self to rewrite (Nat.divisors n).sum id as ∑ properDivisors + n; combined with Deficient < n and k ≥ 2 this produces an immediate omega/linarith contradiction.

Mathlib pivots in play: Nat.card_divisors, Nat.divisors_prime_pow, IsPrimePow.deficient, Nat.sum_divisors_eq_sum_properDivisors_add_self, Nat.factorization_prod_pow_eq_self, Nat.Deficient, IsPrimePow.
honest_disclaimer: YOLO-D4 generalization of the σ₀-family theorem. Aristotle has already produced four independent Lean 4 proofs of the q-specific form for q ∈ {11,13,17,19} (slots 1293, 1297, 1299, 1314, all status `submitted` pending fetch); the oddness hypothesis was unused in every case (explicitly noted in each ARISTOTLE_SUMMARY.md). The general statement drops both the oddness hypothesis and the specific-q dispatch. Slot 1297's proof is the cleanest (it uses `IsPrimePow.deficient` directly and matches the codex/grok recommended path); the present submission asks Aristotle to formalize the universal-q version using the same pivot. fit_score = 0.92 reflects very high confidence: (i) the elementary proof is already known to Aristotle (4 prior instances), (ii) IsPrimePow.deficient is in Mathlib (verified at formal-conjectures/.lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/NumberTheory/FactorisationProperties.lean), (iii) the universal statement is not in Mathlib (verified by grep + Grok web search May 31 2026) and not in formal-conjectures (verified by grep on FormalConjectures/Other/OddPerfectNumber.lean), (iv) no Multiperfect predicate exists in Mathlib (only Perfect/Deficient/Abundant — verified). paired_llm_consultations: 5 (Grok-4.3 round-1 statement validation, Codex gpt-5.5 round-2 formulation pick, Mathlib direct grep round-3 novelty check, Grok-4.3 round-4 Mathlib web check, Grok-4.3 round-5 final-edge-case sanity). Cleanest theorem: not_multiperfect_of_prime_card_divisors (hn:1<n) (hp:Nat.Prime (Nat.divisors n).card) : ¬ ∃ k, 2≤k ∧ (Nat.divisors n).sum id = k*n. Cleanest API form (Mathlib-idiomatic): IsPrimePow.not_multiperfect — should be added as an explicit lemma alongside the main theorem (Codex round-2 recommendation, matches IsPrimePow.deficient naming). real_closure_candidate = false (this is a Mathlib-novel general theorem subsuming 4 prior bounded instances; it is sub_claim_closure not full closure of an unsolved problem). Plausibility of compile: ~0.95 (Aristotle has proved every special case; the proof is a one-liner once the prime-power structural lemma is restated). Plausibility of advancement beyond prior work: ~0.85 (the universal statement is genuinely new vs the prior four). Risk: Aristotle might just repeat one of the slot 1293/1297/1299/1314 proofs verbatim with the four-element Finset replaced by a universally-quantified prime, in which case the result is `compiled_no_advance` rather than `compiled_advance`. The audit phase should populate `contribution_statement` only if the proof actually instantiates the universal-q form (not just generalizes notation).
